channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Bee brains reveal how colonies divide work without central planning - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Bee brains reveal how colonies divide work without central planning  Phys.org
  2. Scientists studying bee brains found clues to how colonies coordinate complex behaviour without a central controller, relying instead on many small individual decisions  The Times of India

item.title: A black hole does not merely bend space; it bends time, pulling it so severely that at the event horizon, time as experienced from the outside appears to stop entirely, meaning something falling in would seem, to a distant observer, to freeze at the edge - Space Daily
item.description: A black hole does not merely bend space; it bends time, pulling it so severely that at the event horizon, time as experienced from the outside appears to stop entirely, meaning something falling in would seem, to a distant observer, to freeze at the edge  Space Daily

item.title: The Longest Journey Sunlight Ever Takes Happens Before It Leaves The Sun - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. The Longest Journey Sunlight Ever Takes Happens Before It Leaves The Sun  ScienceAlert
  2. Most people think the Sun is a vast ball of burning gas, but that familiar picture is quietly wrong: the Sun is plasma, a fourth state of matter — atoms torn into charged particles, the same stuff as lightning and neon  Space Daily
  3. Every second, the Sun converts about 600 million tonnes of hydrogen into helium and loses roughly 4 million tonnes of that mass outright as energy, and the light warming your face today left the core as gamma rays that took somewhere between 10,000 and 1  ScienceBlog.com
  4. Energy created in the Sun’s core can spend around 170,000 years bouncing through the radiative zone before reaching the outer layers — but once released as sunlight, it crosses 150 million kilometres to Earth in just over eight minutes  ScienceBlog.com
  5. You never see the Sun as it is, only as it was: its light takes eight minutes and twenty seconds to cross the 150 million kilometres to Earth — so if the Sun blinked out, we would bask on, unknowing, for eight more minutes  ScienceBlog.com

item.description:

item.title: Does dark energy really exist? Our work identifies cracks in the foundations of today’s cosmological model - The Conversation
item.description:
1. Does dark energy really exist? Our work identifies cracks in the foundations of today’s cosmological model  The Conversation
  2. Einstein’s “Greatest Blunder” May Be Breaking Cosmology  SciTechDaily
  3. We tend to think dark energy has always pushed the universe outward at the same steady rate, but a 2025 survey of 13.1 million galaxies found it appears to have been stronger for the first nine billion years, then began weakening around 4.5 billion years ago  Space Daily

item.description:

item.title: In July 1962, a NASA engineer transcribing the Mariner 1 guidance code missed a single overbar above the letter R, and 293 seconds after launch the rocket veered off course over the Atlantic and had to be destroyed — a hyphen-shaped error that Arthur C. C - Space Daily
